elements.sent-2411-001: This paper introduces the partial Gini covariance, a novel dependence measure that addresses the challenges of high-dimensional inference with heavy-tailed errors, often encountered in fields like finance, insurance, climate, and biology.
elements.sent-2411-002: Conventional high-dimensional regression inference methods suffer from inaccurate type I errors and reduced power in heavy-tailed contexts, limiting their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our proposed approach leverages the partial Gini covariance to construct a robust statistical inference framework that requires minimal tuning and does not impose restrictive moment conditions on error distributions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Unlike traditional methods, it circumvents the need for estimating the density of random errors, and enhances the computational feasibility and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-005: Extensive simulations demonstrate the proposed method’s superior power and robustness over standard high-dimensional inference approaches, such as those based on the debiased Lasso.
elements.sent-2411-006: The asymptotic relative efficiency analysis provides additional theoretical insight on the improved efficiency of the new approach in the heavy-tailed setting.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, the partial Gini covariance extends to the multivariate setting, enabling chi-square testing for a group of coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-008: We illustrate the method’s practical application with a real-world data example.
elements.sent-2411-009: Modern real-world big data, prevalent in fields such as finance, insurance, climate, and biology, often exhibits heavy-tailed behavior \citep{Peinke2004,Ahmad2020,Gupta2022 , characterized by the presence of outliers or extreme values.}
elements.sent-2411-010: When faced with heavy-tailed errors, conventional high-dimensional regression methods frequently yield biased estimates, imprecise inference, and untrustworthy risk assessment.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, \citet{cavanaugh2015probability observed the heavy-tailed phenomenon while analyzing the daily precipitation data from more than 22,000 global stations.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Neglecting the contamination of heavy-tailed random errors in such data analysis could result in an underestimation of the likelihood and severity of the events of interest, thereby significantly impacting disaster preparedness and response strategies.
elements.sent-2411-013: While extensive research efforts have been dedicated to statistical inference with heavy-tailed random errors for low-dimensional data \citep{Kotchoni2012,Davidson2012,Madeira2015 , studies on high-dimensional inference with heavy-tailed errors remain limited.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This is a crucial gap in the literature, as many real-world applications, such as those in finance, insurance, climate, and biology, involve high-dimensional datasets with heavy-tailed behavior.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this paper, we introduce the {\it partial Gini covariance , a novel dependence measure that serves as a foundation for robust statistical inference in high dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Standard high-dimensional inference procedures based on the debiased Lasso \citep{van2014asymptotically, javanmard2014confidence, zhang2014confidence are observed to suffer from inflated type I errors and substantial power loss with heavy-tailed random errors, as demonstrated in the Monte Carlo studies in Section~\ref{sec:4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Our new inference procedure addresses three key challenges.
elements.sent-2411-018: First, it effectively handles heavy-tailed random errors, including Cauchy distribution errors, which are often overlooked in the existing literature on high-dimensional robust regression.
elements.sent-2411-019: Second, it is straightforward to implement with minimal tuning requirements, avoiding the complexities involved in selecting the regularization parameters in high-dimensional regression with heavy-tailed errors.
elements.sent-2411-020: Lastly, it enables valid inference on low-dimensional target regression coefficients without estimating the random errors' density function.
elements.sent-2411-021: Recent advances in robust high-dimensional estimation have primarily focused on Huber regression \citep{loh2021scale,wang2021new,sun2020adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-022: While these approaches relax the sub-Gaussian error assumption, they still require certain moment conditions.
elements.sent-2411-023: For instance, \citet{sun2020adaptive assumes the existence of the $(1+\delta)$-th moment of the random error.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The robust inference procedure developed in \citet{han2022robust requires the existence of the first moment of the random error.}
elements.sent-2411-025: These moment conditions exclude many heavy-tailed distributions, such as Cauchy or Laplace distributions, that are fundamental to traditional robust statistics.
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, high-dimensional Huber-type regression requires tuning two parameters—one for regularization and one for robustification—making it computationally demanding.
elements.sent-2411-027: Consider the high-dimensional linear regression model \begin{equation}\label{equation:linearmodel} Y = \x\trans\bbeta^{\ast} + \varepsilon,\end{equation} where $\x = (X_{1 , \ldots, X_{p )\trans$ is the $p$-dimensional vector of covariates, $\bbeta^{\ast = (\beta_{1 ^{\ast ,\ldots, \beta_{p ^{\ast )\trans\in \mR^{p}$ is the $p$-dimensional coefficient, $\varepsilon$ denotes the random error independent of $\x$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Without loss of generality, we assume $\E(\x) = \vz_{p}$, where $\vz_{p}$ denotes the $p$-dimensional zero vector and the intercept is absorbed into $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The number of covariates $p$ may substantially exceed the sample size $n$, and notably we do not impose any moment condition on $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-030: For an arbitrary $1\leq k\leq p$, we aim to test the significance of target parameter $\beta_{k ^{\ast}$ in the presence of the high-dimensional nuisance parameter $\bbeta^{\ast _{-k}\defby(\beta_{1 ^{\ast ,\ldots,\beta_{k-1 ^{\ast ,\beta_{k+1 ^{\ast ,\ldots,\beta_{p ^{\ast )\trans$, and extend this to simultaneous testing the significance of a group of coefficients.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The observed data consist of $\{(Y_{i , \x_{i )\}_{i=1 ^{n}$ with $Y_{i = \x_{i}\trans\bbeta^{\ast + \varepsilon_{i}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The Gini covariance is a useful measure of the dependence between random variables from heavy-tailed distributions, and it is widely applied in areas such as economics (e.g., studying the relationship between income and expenditure) and social sciences \citep{yitzhaki2013gini .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this paper, we introduce the concept of partial Gini covariance, partly inspired by the Rank Lasso procedure \citep{wang2020tuning which incorporates the Gini covariance \citep{schezhtman1987measure into its loss function for robust estimation.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We show that the partial Gini covariance, introduced in Section 2, acts as a robust decorrelated score function satisfying Neyman's orthogonality condition, and serves as the foundation for robust statistical inference in high-dimensional regression.
elements.sent-2411-036: We propose an estimator for the partial Gini covariance in the high-dimensional setting, and based on the estimation, we construct a hypothesis testing procedure and investigate the limiting distributions of the proposed test statistic under both the null hypothesis and the local alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our approach differs from inference methods based on Huber regression in that it does not require moment conditions on random errors and it circumvents the complexity of simultaneously tuning two regularization parameters.
elements.sent-2411-038: Unlike the debiased Rank Lasso method, which is informally discussed in \cite{fan2020comment for the low-dimensional scenario, we address the more challenging high-dimensional inference problem.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Our proposed statistic converges to a normal distribution, and its implementation does not need to estimate the density of the random error.
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, by exploring the double summation expression of the partial Gini covariance, we have considerably relaxed the constraints on the sample size $n$ and the dimension $p$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Existing quantile-based debiased methods implicitly require $p = o\{\exp(n^{1/5 )\}$ when the random errors follow a sub-Gaussian distribution \citep{belloni2014uniform, zhao2014general, bradic2017uniform , whereas our method only requires $p = o\{\exp(n^{1/3 )\}$, despite the discontinuous nature of the sample loss function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Our requirement is also less stringent than that for the debiased Huber regression, which requires $p = o\{\exp(n^{\alpha/3 )\}$ for some $\alpha\in(0,1)$ \citep{han2022robust .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, we provide the asymptotic relative efficiency (ARE) between the proposed method and the existing high-dimensional inference methods including \cite{zhang2014confidence,ning2017general,ma2017variable .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Our ARE analysis provides additional theoretical insight on the improved efficiency of our approach in the heavy-tailed setting.
elements.sent-2411-045: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:power , the efficiency improvement can be substantial, with power performance exceeding four times those of the existing methods when the random error follows a Cauchy distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, we extend the concept of partial Gini covariance to the multivariate case and establish an asymptotic chi-square test to test the significance of a multivariate coefficient vector.
elements.sent-2411-047: We conduct extensive simulation studies and compare our method with three prominent existing methods, including the debiased Lasso \citep{van2014asymptotically, javanmard2014confidence, zhang2014confidence , the partial Pearson covariance-based method \citep{ning2017general and partial quantile covariance-based methods \citep{li2015quantile,ma2017variable .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our results demonstrate that our approach exhibits superior robustness and efficiency in handling high-dimensional data with heavy-tailed random errors.
elements.sent-2411-049: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-050: Section \ref{subsec:2.1 introduces the concept of partial Gini covariance and establish the corresponding estimation method.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In Section \ref{sec:2 , we propose a statistical inference procedure for high-dimensional linear regression and establish asymptotic properties of the test statistic under both the null and alternative hypotheses.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Furthermore, we extend the notion of partial Gini covariance to the multivariate scenario and construct a chi-square test.
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section \ref{sec:3 , we compare our methods with standard approaches for high-dimensional inference and analyze the asymptotic relative efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Section \ref{sec:4 provides comprehensive simulation studies for testing the significance of both univariate and multivariate coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We illustrate the application of the new methods using a real-world data example in Section \ref{sec:5 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Technical proofs are deferred to the Supplementary Material.
elements.sent-2411-057: To construct a test statistic that effectively handles heavy-tailed errors in the high-dimensional setting, we introduce a novel measure called the partial Gini covariance in Section~\ref{PGC1 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: We then discuss how to estimate the partial Gini covariance in high dimensional settings in Section~\ref{PGC2 .}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Partial Gini Covariance and Neyman Orthogonality
elements.sent-2411-059: To introduce the notion of partial Gini covariance, we first recall that the Gini covariance between two random variables $Y$ and $X$ is defined as \beqrs \Gcov(Y,X) \defby \cov\left\{ F_{Y (Y),X\right\}, \eeqrs where $F_Y(y)$ is the cumulative distribution function of $Y$, and $\cov(\cdot, \cdot)$ denotes the covariance function.}
elements.sent-2411-060: It is the basic building block for Gini regression \citep{olkin1992gini , the loss function of which can be written as \beqrs \Gcov(Y-\bbeta\trans\x,Y-\bbeta\trans\x) =\frac{1 {4 \E\big\{ \abs{(Y_{i -\bbeta\trans\x_{i ) - (Y_{j -\bbeta\trans\x_{j )}\big\}, \eeqrs where $1\leq i, j \leq n$; $Y_{i}$ and $Y_{j}$ are independent copies of $Y$; $\x_{i}$ and $\x_{j}$ are independent copies of $\x$ (e.g., \citet{lerman1984 , Section 2.1.3 of \citet{yitzhaki2013gini ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Gini regression is known to be equivalent to the popular rank regression loss function with the Wilcoxon score function \citep{jaeckel1972,Hettmansperger1998 , which enjoys superior robustness and efficiency properties.}
elements.sent-2411-062: For $1\leq k\leq p$, we denote $\z_{k}\defby \x_{-k}$, where $\x_{-k = (X_{1 ,\ldots, X_{k-1 , X_{k+1 ,\ldots,X_{p )\trans$, for notational simplicity.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: To measure the dependence between $Y$ and $X_{k}$ adjusting for the confounder $\z_{k}$ in the heavy-tailed case, we introduce an intuitive new concept, {\it partial Gini covariance .}
elements.sent-2411-064: We first regress $X_{k}$ on $\z_{k}$ and denote $\bgamma_{k = \arg\min_{\bgamma}\E\left\{(X_{k - \bgamma\trans\z_{k )^2\right\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Then, we regress $Y$ on $\z_{k}$ using Gini regression and denote $\btheta_{G,k =\arg\min_{\btheta \E\left\{(Y - \btheta\trans\z_{k ) F_{ Y - \btheta\trans\z_{k ( Y \right.$ $\left.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The partial Gini covariance between $Y$ and $X_{k}$ given $\z_{k}$ is defined as \beqr \pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) = \cov\left\{ F_{Y - \btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{k (Y -\btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{k ), X_{k - \bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{k}\right\}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: We observe that $\btheta_{G,k}$ satisfies an important moment condition when $E(\z_{k ) = \vz_{p-1}$ and $\varepsilon_{1 -\varepsilon_{2}$ follows continuous distribution: \beqr \E\left\{\z_{k F_{ Y - \btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{k ( Y - \btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{k )\right\} = \vz_{p-1 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: As a result, the Gini covariance between $Y - \btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{k}$ and covariate $\z_{k}$ equals zero.
elements.sent-2411-069: It is clear that $Y$ has a bounded influence for the partial Gini covariance as its effect is through $F(\cdot)$, which is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-070: Meanwhile, Lemma \ref{lemma:neyman_orthogonality below shows the partial Gini covariance in \eqref{equation:partailgini enjoys a property called Neyman orthogonality, which ensures small errors in estimating the nuisance parameters do not significantly affect the estimation of the parameter of interest \citep{Neyman1959, Neyman1979, chernozhukov2015valid .}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The bounded influence and the Neyman orthogonality together provide the foundation for robust inference in high dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-073: When $\beta_{k ^{\ast = 0$, we have \beqrs \frac{\partial M(\btheta, \bgamma) {\partial \btheta \Big\lvert_{(\btheta,\bgamma) = (\btheta_{G,k , \bgamma_{k ) = \vz_{p-1 ~ and ~ \frac{\partial M(\btheta, \bgamma) {\partial \bgamma \Big\lvert_{(\btheta,\bgamma) = (\btheta_{G,k , \bgamma_{k ) = \vz_{p-1 .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: As the result, the estimate for the partial Gini covariance can still achieve root-$n$ asymptotic normality even when the estimation bias measured in the $L_2$ norm of $\btheta_{G,k}$ and $\bgamma_{k}$ is of order $O\{(n^{-1/2}\log p)^{1/2}\}$ in high dimensions.
elements.subsection-2411-002: The Estimation of Partial Gini Covariance in High Dimensions
elements.sent-2411-075: We are interested in the high-dimensional scenario where $p$ is much larger than $n$ ($p\gg n$).
elements.sent-2411-076: To overcome the overfitting due to high dimensionality, we use regularized estimates with sparsity assumptions on both $\boldsymbol{\theta _{G,k}$ and $\boldsymbol{\gamma _{k}$.. Estimation of $\btheta_{G,k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: We adopt the regularized Gini regression (or equivalently rank regression) to estimate $\btheta_{G,k}$ in the high-dimensional setting.
elements.sent-2411-078: Specifically, the estimate of $\btheta_{G,k}$ is defined as $\wh\btheta_{G,k}\defby \arg\min_{\btheta}\calL_{1 (Y, \z_{k ; \btheta)$, where $\btheta=(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{p-1 )\trans$ and $\calL_{1 (Y, \z_{k ; \btheta)$ is the regularized Gini loss function given by \beqr \calL_{1}\left(Y, \z_{k ; \btheta\right) = \{n(n-1)\}^{-1 \sum_{i=1 ^{n}\sum_{j\neq i ^{n |(Y_i-\btheta\trans\z_{k,i ) -(Y_j-\btheta\trans\z_{k,j )| + \lambda_{Y,G,k}\sum_{l=1 ^{p-1}\abs{\theta_{l .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Rank Lasso enjoys two particularly appealing properties.
elements.sent-2411-080: First, it possesses desirable robustness and efficiency in the presence of heavy-tailed error contamination.
elements.sent-2411-081: Second, its implementation is free of tuning parameter in the sense that $\lambda_{Y,G,k}$ could be simulated without knowledge of the random error distribution.
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, let $\s_{n = -2\{n(n-1)\}^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n}\z_{k,i r_{i}$ denote the subgradient, with $\{r_1,\ldots,r_{n}\}$ following the uniform distribution on the permutations of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: It is noted that the distribution of $\s_{n}$ does not depend on the error distribution.. Estimation of $\bgamma_{k$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The estimate of $\bgamma_{k}$ is defined as $\wh\bgamma_{k}\defby \arg\min_{\bgamma \calL_{2}\left(X_{k , \z_{k ; \bgamma\right)$, where \beqrs \calL_{2}\left(X_{k , \z_{k ; \bgamma\right) \defby (2n)^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (X_{k,i - \bgamma\trans\z_{k,i )^2 + \lambda_{X,k}\sum_{l=1 ^{p-1}\abs{\gamma_{l .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Estimation of the partial Gini covariance.
elements.sent-2411-086: Denote $\wh\varepsilon_{k,i \defby Y_{i - \wh\btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{k,i}$ and let $R(\wh\varepsilon_{k,i )$ be the rank of $\wh\varepsilon_{k,i}$ among $\{\wh\varepsilon_{k,i , i=1,\ldots,n\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Our estimate for $\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ is \beqr \wh\pGcov( Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) \defby n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (X_{k,i - \wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{k,i )\{R(\wh\varepsilon_{k,i )/n - 1/2\} = n^{-2}\sum_{i=1 ^{n}\sum_{j=1 ^{n}\left(X_{k,i - \wh{\bgamma}\trans\z_{k,i}\right) \left\{ 1\left(\wh\varepsilon_{k,i \geq \wh\varepsilon_{k,j \right) - 1/2\right\}.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Furthermore, the estimate is computationally efficient.
elements.sent-2411-089: Denote $s_{X \defby \max_{1\leq k\leq p \|\bgamma_{k}\|_{0}$ and $s_{Y,G \defby \max_{1\leq k\leq p \|\btheta_{G,k}\|_{0}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: When the covariates follow the sub-Gaussian distribution, $\|\wh\btheta_{G,k - \btheta_{G,k}\|_{2 = O_p(\lambda_{Y,G,k s_{Y,G ^{1/2 )$ and $\lambda_{Y,G,k =O\{(n^{-1}\log p)^{1/2 \}$ under the lower restricted eigenvalue condition and the random error condition \citep{wang2020tuning .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Under the lower restricted eigenvalue condition, $\|\bgamma_{k - \bgamma_{k}\|_{1 = O_p(\lambda_{X,k s_{X )$, where $\lambda_{X,k =O\{(n^{-1}\log p)^{1/2 \}$ \citep{fan2020statistical .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: We relegate the lower restricted eigenvalue condition and the random error condition to the Supplementary Material.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Testing the Significance of a Target Covariate
elements.sent-2411-093: We consider the target parameter of interest $\beta_{k ^{\ast}$, with $\z_{k}$ representing the vector of high-dimensional confounders.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Our main objective is to test the following hypothesis: \begin{equation}\label{test} H_{0,k}: \beta_k^{\ast} = 0\quad v.s \quad H_{1,k}: \beta_k^{\ast} \neq 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: We impose the following regularity conditions to facilitate our technical derivation.
elements.sent-2411-096: Let $F_{\varepsilon_{1 - \varepsilon_{2 (\cdot)$ denote the distribution function of $\varepsilon_{1 - \varepsilon_{2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: There exists some constant $c_{1 >0$ such that $\E\{\exp(t\v\trans\x)\}\leq \exp(c_{1 t^2/2)$ for each fixed $\v\in \calS^{d-1}$ and all $t \in \mR$, where $\calS^{d-1}$ is the surface of $d$-dimensional unit ball.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Condition \ref{condition:continuous_error and its strengthen version Condition \ref{condition:error_distribution allow the random error $\epsilon$ to follow a heavy-tailed distribution such as the Cauchy distribution, relaxing the standard normality or sub-gaussianity assumption on $\epsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Condition \ref{condition:sub_gaussion states that the covariates follow the sub-Gaussian distribution, which is common in high-dimensional inference literature \citep{javanmard2014confidence,ning2017general .}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Condition \ref{condition:order also provides relations among the sample size, the dimension of the data, and the sparsity levels of the regression coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-101: When $s_{X}$ and $s_{G,Y}$ are finite, this condition is equivalent to $\log(p) = o(n^{1/3 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Compared with the conditions for the inference of high-dimensional quantile regression models \citep{zhao2014general,belloni2014uniform , which require $\log(p) = o(n^{1/5 )$ under the sub-Gaussian setting, the condition in our context is notably less restrictive.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This relaxation is facilitated by the double summation form in \eqref{equation:giniestimate , see the proof of Theorem \ref{theorem:normality .}}
elements.sent-2411-104: We first demonstrate that the testing problem in \eqref{test is equivalent to the following hypothesis testing problem based on the partial Gini covariance: \beqr H_{0,k ^{G : \pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) = 0\quad v.s \quad H_{1,k ^{G : \pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) \neq 0.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: If $\beta_{k ^{\ast = 0$, the derivative of $\Gcov(Y - \boldsymbol{\theta ^\top\z_{k - bX_{k , Y - \boldsymbol{\theta ^\top\z_{k - bX_{k )$ with respect to $b$ at $b=0$ equals zero.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Following the similar derivation as that in Section S.1 of the Supplementary Material, we can show that \beqrs \E \left\{\frac{\partial\Gcov(Y - \btheta\trans\z_{k - bX_{k , Y - \btheta\trans\z_{k - bX_{k ) {\partial b \Big\lvert_{(\btheta,b) = (\btheta_{G,k , 0) \right\} = -4 \cdot \pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The the equivalence between \eqref{test and \eqref{pctest is formally stated in Lemma \ref{lemma:equalb below.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Let $f_{\varepsilon_1-\varepsilon_2 (\cdot)$ be the density function of $\varepsilon_{1 - \varepsilon_{2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Theorem~\ref{theorem:normality below states the asymptotic normality of $\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ under both the null and local alternative hypotheses.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: We also assume that \beqr \|\wh\btheta_{G,k}\|_{0}\leq c_{2 s_{Y,G \eeqr and $\|\bgamma_{k}\|_{2}$ is bounded.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Under $H_{0,k}$ in \eqref{test , $n^{1/2}\wh\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )\stackrel{d {\longrightarrow {\cal N (0, \sigma_{G,k ^2) $ as $n\to\infty$, where $``\stackrel{d {\longrightarrow "$ stands for ``converges in distribution".}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Under $H_{1,k, n : \beta_{k ^{\ast = n^{-1/2}\beta_{k,0 with \beta_{k,0 \neq 0$, $n^{1/2}\wh\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) \stackrel{d {\longrightarrow \calN( f_{\varepsilon_{1 - \varepsilon_{2 (0)\beta_{k,0 \E \left\{ (X_{k -\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{k )^2 \right\} , \sigma_{G,k ^2)$, as $n\to\infty$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Assumption \eqref{equation:se is commonly adopted for high-dimensional statistical inference \citep{guo2024model,zhao2014general .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: For rank Lasso, we verify that \eqref{equation:se holds with high probability in Section \ref{section:se_condition of the Supplementary Material.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: It follows from Theorem \ref{theorem:normality that the asymptotic variance of partial Gini covariance is $\sigma_{G,k ^2=12^{-1}\E\left\{\left(X_{ki - \bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki}\right)^2\right\}$ under $H_{0,k}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: This result has two important implications.
elements.sent-2411-119: First, the asymptotic variance $\sigma_{G,k ^2$ is independent of the distribution of $Y$, allowing for $Y$ with a heavy-tailed distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Thus it ensures the robustness of the test based on the partial Gini covariance.
elements.sent-2411-121: Second, the result eliminates the need for error density estimation, distinct from the robust test statistics based on debiased rank Lasso estimator \citep{fan2020comment , where the asymptotic variance depends on the density of the random error.}
elements.sent-2411-122: To conduct the hypothesis test \eqref{test , we employ $\wh\sigma_{G,k ^2 \defby (12n)^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n}\left(X_{ki - \wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki}\right)^2$ as the estimate of $\sigma_{G,k ^2$.}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-004: Simultaneous Testing the Significance of a Group of Covariates
elements.sent-2411-128: Let $\mathcal{S = \{k_{1 ,\ldots, k_{d}\}$ denote the index set of the parameters of interest, where $d\defby\abs{\mathcal{S }$ denotes the cardinality of the set $\mathcal{S}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Let $\boldsymbol{\beta _{\mathcal{S ^{\ast = (\beta_{k_{1 ^{\ast ,\ldots, \beta_{k_{d ^{\ast )\trans$ represent the corresponding sub-vector of $\boldsymbol{\beta ^{\ast}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: We now consider testing if these coefficients are simultaneously zero: \beqr H_{0,\calS : \bbeta_{\calS ^{\ast = \vz_{d ,\quad v.s \quad H_{1,\calS : \bbeta_{\calS ^{\ast}\neq \vz_{d .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Let $\btheta_{G,\calS}\defby \arg\min\limits_{\btheta\in\mR^{p-d \E \left\{(Y - \btheta\trans\z_{\calS ) F_{ Y - \btheta\trans\z_{\calS ( Y - \btheta\trans\z_{\calS )\right\}$ and $\bGam_{\calS}\defby \arg\min\limits_{\bGam\in\mR^{(p-d)\times d }\E\big(\|\x_{\calS - \bGam\trans\z_{\calS )\|^2_{2}\big)$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Lemma~\ref{lemma:equal_multi below is an extension of Lemma~\ref{lemma:equalb .}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Based on Lemma \ref{lemma:equal_multi , testing \eqref{equation:multihypo is equivalent to testing \beqrs H_{0,\calS ^{G : \pGcov(Y, \x_{\calS}\mid \z_{\calS )= \vz_{d , \quad v.s \quad H_{1,\calS ^{G : \pGcov(Y, \x_{\calS}\mid \z_{\calS )\neq \vz_{d .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: To estimate $\bGam_{\calS}$, we use node-wise least square regression with Lasso penalty \citep{van2014asymptotically, javanmard2014confidence, zhang2014confidence .}
elements.sent-2411-136: For $j=1,\ldots,d$, by using Lasso penalty with the tuning parameter $\lambda_{X,k_{j }$, \beqrs \wh\bgamma_{k_{j }\defby \arg\min_{\bgamma (2n)^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n \|X_{k_{j ,i - \bgamma\trans\z_{\calS,i}\|_{2 ^2 + \lambda_{X,k_{j }\|\bgamma\|_{1 .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Based on \cite{wang2020tuning , we have $\|\wh\btheta_{G,\calS - \btheta_{G,\calS}\|_{2 = O_p(\lambda_{Y,G,\calS s_{Y,G ^{1/2 )$, where $\lambda_{Y,G,\calS =O\{(n^{-1}\log p)^{1/2 \}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: According to \cite{fan2020statistical , we have $\|\wh\bGam_{\calS - \bGam_{\calS}\|_{1 = O_p(\lambda_{X,\calS s_{X )$, where $\lambda_{X,\calS}\defby \max\{\lambda_{X, k_{j , j=1,\ldots, d\}= O\{(n^{-1}\log p)^{1/2 \}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: The estimate for $\pGcov(Y, X_{\calS}\mid\z_{\calS )$ is \beqrs \wh\pGcov(Y, \x_{\calS}\mid\z_{\calS ) \defby n^{-2}\sum_{i=1 ^{n}\sum_{j=1 ^{n}\left(\x_{\calS,i - \wh\bGam_{\calS}\trans\z_{\calS,i}\right) \left\{ 1\left(\wh\varepsilon_{\calS,i \geq \wh\varepsilon_{\calS,j \right) - 1/2\right\}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: The asymptotic distributions for $\wh{W _{G,\calS}$ under both the null hypothesis and the local alternatives are given in Theorem \ref{theorem:chi_square below.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Under $H_{0,\calS}$, $n\wh{W _{G,\calS}\stackrel{d {\longrightarrow}\chi_{d ^{2 $, as $n\to\infty$, where $\chi_{d ^{2}$ stands for the chi-square distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom;.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Under $H_{1,\calS, n : \bbeta_{\calS ^{\ast = n^{-1/2}\bbeta_{\calS,0 with \bbeta_{\calS,0 \neq \vz_{d}$, as $n\to\infty$, \beqrs n\wh{W _{G,\calS}\stackrel{d {\longrightarrow}\chi_{d ^{2}\big\{ 144f_{\varepsilon_{1 - \varepsilon_{2 (0)^2 \bbeta_{\calS,0}\trans\bSig_{G,\calS}\bbeta_{\calS,0}\big\}, \eeqrs where $\chi_{d ^{2 (\mu)$ is the noncentral chi-square distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom and non-centrality parameter $\mu$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Theorem \ref{theorem:chi_square implies that $\wh{W _{G,\calS}$ has nontrivial power in detecting the local alternatives even if $\bbeta_{\calS ^{\ast}$ converges to $0$ at the rate of $n^{-1/2}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: When $\bbeta_{\calS ^{\ast}$ is a nonzero vector of constants (or fixed alternative), the power of the above test converges to one as $n$ diverges.}
elements.section-2411-004: Power Advantages for Heavy-tailed Error Distributions: Comparison with Existing Methods
elements.sent-2411-146: In this section, we conduct a detailed power comparison between our proposed testing method and those utilizing partial Pearson covariance and partial quantile covariance.
elements.sent-2411-147: Our analysis reveals that the proposed method can be substantially more efficient than the partial Pearson covariance approach, particularly in scenarios with heavy-tailed random errors.
elements.sent-2411-148: Furthermore, the analysis suggests greater power than the method based on partial quantile covariance under local alternatives.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Comparison with the Method Based on Partial Pearson Covariance
elements.sent-2411-149: We derive the efficiency gain of the new test based on the partial Gini covariance versus the one based on partial Pearson covariance in the presence of heavy-tailed random errors.
elements.sent-2411-150: The partial Pearson covariance, proposed by \cite{yule1897theory , is a classical tool for testing the significance of the coefficient in the linear model.}
elements.sent-2411-151: In the high-dimensional settings, the estimate for partial Pearson covariance is given by \beqr \wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) = n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (Y_{i -\wh\btheta_{P,k}\trans\z_{ki )(X_{ki -\wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki ), \eeqr where $\wh\btheta_{P,k = \arg\min_{\btheta (2n)^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (Y_{i - \btheta\trans\z_{k,i )^2 + \lambda_{Y,P,k}\|\btheta\|_{1}$ with $\lambda_{Y,P,k}$ being the tuning parameter.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Under the null hypothesis, \eqref{equation:partialpearson is asymptotically equivalent to the test statistic proposed by \cite{zhang2014confidence .}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Therefore, the following comparison also includes some earlier testing methods including \cite{zhang2014confidence,van2014asymptotically,javanmard2014confidence .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Meanwhile, \eqref{equation:partialpearson is also equivalent to the decorrelated score test of \cite{ning2017general for the linear regression model.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: They further proved that $n^{1/2}\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ converges to the normal distribution with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{P,k ^2\defby \E(\varepsilon^2)\cdot \E\big\{(X_{k -\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{k )^2\big\}$ under $H_{0,k}$, and proposed to test \eqref{test based on the statistic $n^{1/2}\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )/\wh\sigma_{P,k}$, where $\wh\sigma_{P,k ^2\defby \big\{n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (Y_{i -\wh\btheta_{P,k}\trans\z_{ki )^2\big\}\big\{n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (X_{ki -\wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki )^2\big\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: It is important to note that $n^{1/2}\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )/\wh\sigma_{P,k}$ is only applicable to test \eqref{test when $E(\epsilon^2)$ exists.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Thus, $\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ may not perform well when $\epsilon$ is heavy-tailed.}
elements.sent-2411-158: To compare the performance of the tests using $\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ and $\wh\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ for \eqref{test , we derive Pitman's asymptotic relative efficiency (ARE) \citep{pitman1948lecture, noether1955theorem of these two tests.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Pitman's ARE is a useful measure for comparing the performance of two statistical tests in large samples.
elements.sent-2411-160: It is defined as the ratio of the sample sizes needed by two tests to achieve the same power under a sequence of local alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-162: The Pitman sequence of the local alternatives is given by: \begin{equation}H_{1,k,n} : \beta_{k}^{\ast} = n^{-1/2}\beta_{k,0},\end{equation} where $\beta_{k,0 > 0$ is a constant.}
elements.sent-2411-163: For ease of presentation, we assume that $\z_{k}$ and $X_{k}$ are independent of each other.
elements.sent-2411-164: Under this condition, we can derive Pitman's ARE as follows: \beqr \ARE\Big(\wh\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ), \wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) \Big) = 12\var(\epsilon)f_{\varepsilon_{1 - \varepsilon_{2 ^2(0).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: In Table \ref{table:are , we present the values of ARE with different distributions of $\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-166: It can be observed that $\wh\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ demonstrates comparable performance with $\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ when $\epsilon$ follows the normal distribution while exhibits significantly higher power when $\epsilon$ is heavy-tailed.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \captionsetup{font = footnotesize} \caption{ { The asymptotic relative efficiency for $\wh\pGcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k})$ and $\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k})$ under different distributions of $\varepsilon$. We consider 5 different error distributions including, Normal(0,1): standard normal distribution; Uniform[0,1]: uniform distribution on $[0,1]$; T$_{3}$(0,1): $t$-distribution with 3 degrees of freedom; Exp(1)" exponential distribution with rate $1$; and LogNormal(0,1): log-normal distribution. } } \label{table:are} 1{ \begin{tabular}{lllllll} $\varepsilon$ Normal(0,1) Uniform[0,1] T$_{3}$(0,1) Exp(1) LogNormal(0,1) ARE 0.955 1.000 1.901 3.000 7.353 \end{tabular}}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Comparison with the Method Based on Partial Quantile Covariance
elements.sent-2411-167: In the context of robust regression, quantile-based methods are among the most common approaches.
elements.sent-2411-168: Several studies have investigated variable selection within these methods \citep{wu2009variable,wang2012quantile,lee2014model .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Notably, significant advancements have been made by \citet{li2015quantile and \citet{ma2017variable , who introduced the concept of partial quantile covariance to quantify the interplay between variables $Y$ and $X_{k}$, while taking into account the confounding influence of covariates $\mathbf{z _{k}\in\mathbb{R ^{p-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: The partial quantile covariance is defined as follows: \beqrs \pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) = \cov\left\{ \psi_{\tau (Y - \btheta_{Q,k}\trans\z_{k - Q_{\tau, Y-\btheta_{Q,k}\trans\z_{k ), X_{k - \bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{k}\right\}, \eeqrs where $\btheta_{Q,k}\defby\arg\min_{(\btheta,a)}\E\{\rho_{\tau (Y - \btheta\trans\z_{k - Q_{\tau, Y-\btheta\trans\z_{k )\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: The partial quantile covariance and partial Gini covariance are closely related.
elements.sent-2411-172: To bridge these two measures, we introduce an intermediate concept.
elements.sent-2411-173: Inspired by \citet{zou2008composite , who proposed composite quantile regression to simultaneously consider $T$ levels of quantile $\tau_{t}\in[0,1]$ for $t=1,\ldots,T$ simultaneously, we define the partial composite quantile covariance as \beqrs \pCQcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) \defby \cov\left\{ T^{-1}\sum_{t=1 ^{T}\psi_{\tau_{t (Y - \btheta_{CQ,k}\trans\z_{k - Q_{\tau_{t , Y-\btheta_{CQ,k}\trans\z_{k ), X_{k - \bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{k}\right\}, \eeqrs where $\btheta_{CQ,k}\defby\arg\min_{\btheta}\E\{T^{-1}\sum_{t=1 ^{T}\rho_{\tau_{t (Y - \btheta\trans\z - Q_{\tau_{t , Y-\btheta\trans\z_{k )\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: In the case where $\tau_{t = t/(T+1)$, we can establish Proposition \ref{proposition:relationship .}}
elements.sent-2411-175: In this study, we aim to formulate the asymptotic normality of the partial quantile covariance in the high dimensional case, extending the work of \cite{li2015quantile which was limited to the case where \( p \) is finite.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Denote \beqr (\wh{Q _{\tau, Y - \btheta_{Q, k}\trans\z_{k ,\wh\btheta_{Q, k )\defby \arg\min_{\eta,\btheta n^{-1 \sum_{i=1 ^{n \rho_{\tau (Y_{i - \btheta\trans\z_{ki - \eta) + \lambda_{Y,Q,k}\sum_{l=1 ^{p-1}\abs{\theta_{l , \eeqr where $\lambda_{Y,Q,k}$ is the tuning parameter.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Then we define the estimate for partial quantile covariance in high-dimensional settings is \beqrs \wh\pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) = n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n}\psi_{\tau (Y_{i -\z_{ki}\trans\wh\btheta_{Q,k - \wh{Q _{\tau, Y - \btheta_{Q, k}\trans\z_{k ) (X_{ki -\wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki ).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: We impose the following conditions to establish the asymptotic normality of $\wh\pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$:.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Condition \ref{condition:error_bound_quantile includes the estimation error bound of $\wh\btheta_{Q,k}$ derived in the previous literature \citep{belloni2011l1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: A similar condition is imposed in \cite{zhao2014general for testing high dimensional composite quantile regression coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-181: When $ s_{X}$ and $s_{Q,Y}$ are finite, Condition \ref{condition:order_quantile is equivalent to $\log p = o(n^{1/5 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: This condition has been imposed in \citet{belloni2014uniform and \citet{zhao2014general , which focus on the inference of the high-dimensional quantile regression model when $\epsilon$ is sub-Gaussian.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: To conduct hypothesis test, we determine whether to reject $H_{0,k}$ in \eqref{test based on the value of $n^{1/2}\wh\pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) /\wh\sigma_{Q,k}$, where $\wh\sigma_{Q,k ^2\defby (\tau - \tau^2)\big\{n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (X_{ki -\wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki )^2\big\}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: This test, using \( n^{1/2}\wh\pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )/\wh\sigma_{Q,k \) for \eqref{test , is more restrictive than the proposed test based on partial Gini covariance, which requires \( \log(p) = o(n^{1/3 ) \), as it necessitates \( \log(p) = o(n^{1/5 ) \).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: An issue arises when assessing the effectiveness of $\wh\pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Consider a specific scenario where both $X_{k}$ and $\varepsilon$ follow the standard normal distributions, and $X_{k}$ is independent with $\x_{-k}$.
elements.sent-2411-191: Rather than calculating $\mu_{Q ^{\prime (0)$, we investigate the limiting behavior of $\mu_{Q ^{\prime (n^{-1/2}\beta_{k,0 )$ as $n\to\infty$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: This aspect has not been previously addressed in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-194: We illustrate the empirical power for $\wh\pQcov_{\tau (Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ in Section \ref{subsec:4.1 , which indeed confirms this observation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: The derivation of Equation \eqref{equation:are_quantile is provided in the Supplementary Material.}
elements.sent-2411-196: In this section, we present the finite sample performance of the partial Gini covariance-based test.
elements.sent-2411-197: We consider two studies for our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-198: The first study aims to examine the empirical size of the partial Gini covariance-based test and partial quantile covariance-based test.
elements.sent-2411-199: The second study focuses on comparing the power performance of other high-dimensional independence tests against others.
elements.sent-2411-200: Throughout the simulations, we generate $\x_{i = (X_{i1 ,\ldots, X_{ip )\trans$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ independently from multivariate normal distribution with mean zero and covariance $\bSig$, where $(\bSig)_{s,t = 0.5^{\abs{s-t }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: We consider the following linear model, for $i=1,\ldots,n$, \beqrs Y_{i = \x_{i}\trans\bbeta^{\ast + \varepsilon_{i .}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: We consider three different noise settings: (i) $\varepsilon\sim \calN(0,1)$, (ii) $\varepsilon\sim T_{2 (0,1)$, (iii) $\varepsilon\sim Cauchy(0,1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-203: The latter two are both heavy-tailed distributions.
elements.sent-2411-204: We fix $n=200$ and consider $p=500$ and $p=2000$ under three different distributions of $\varepsilon_{i}$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-205: We compare the empirical distributions of six test statistics in the following context, which can be divided into two types.
elements.sent-2411-206: The first type is partial covariance-based tests, involving partial Gini covariance-based test statistic, quantile partial covariance-based test statistic with $\tau = 0.5$, and partial Pearson covariance-based test statistic.
elements.sent-2411-207: To ensure that linear coefficients $\btheta_{P,k}$ are correctly specified in partial Pearson covariance under heavy-tailed cases, we also implement another modified estimate by applying rank Lasso \citep{wang2020tuning .}
elements.sent-2411-208: The estimate is denoted as \beqrs \wt\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k ) = n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (Y_{i -\wh\btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{ki )(X_{ki -\wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki ), \eeqrs The corresponding test statistic is $n^{1/2}\wt\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )/\wt\sigma_{P,k}$, where $\wt\sigma_{P,k ^2\defby \big\{n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (Y_{i -\wh\btheta_{G,k}\trans\z_{ki )^2\big\}\big\{n^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n (X_{ki -\wh\bgamma_{k}\trans\z_{ki )^2\big\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: The second group includes debiased Lasso \citep{zhang2014confidence and its bootstrap version \cite{dezeure2017high .}}
elements.sent-2411-210: These two test statistics are implemented using R package hdi with $101$ times of bootstrap.. Study 1.
elements.sent-2411-211: In this study, we examine the empirical sizes of the aforementioned test statistics.
elements.sent-2411-213: We test the hypothesis $H_{0,11}$, which represents the independent case.
elements.sent-2411-214: Here, the significant level $\alpha$ is set to $0.05$.
elements.sent-2411-215: The empirical sizes under six scenarios are presented in Table \ref{table:size .}
elements.sent-2411-216: From the table, it is evident that the sizes of the proposed test and the test based on partial quantile covariance are well-controlled at approximately $0.05$ under three different noise settings.
elements.sent-2411-217: In contrast, both of the two partial Pearson covariance-based test statistics are overly conservative when $\varepsilon$ follows heavy-tailed distributions.
elements.sent-2411-218: In fact, partial Pearson covariance-based test statistics failed to converge to the normal distribution, because the asymptotic variances of estimated partial Pearson covariance $n^{1/2}\wh\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$ and $n^{1/2}\wt\pPcov(Y, X_{k}\mid \z_{k )$, do not exist.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: As for two debiased Lasso-based test statistics, the empirical sizes cannot be well-controlled when $\varepsilon$ follows heavy-tailed distributions.
elements.sent-2411-220: For instance, when $(n,p)=(200, 2000)$ and $\varepsilon$ follow $t$ distribution with $2$ degrees of freedom, the empirical size of debiased Lasso-based tests reach $0.100$.
elements.sent-2411-221: This observation highlights the importance of employing rank-based partial covariance to achieve the asymptotic normality of the test.. Study 2.
elements.sent-2411-222: We examine the empirical power performance of the test statistics in Study 1.
elements.sent-2411-224: We keep $\beta_{2 ^{\ast =\ldots=\beta_{10 ^{\ast =1$ and vary $\beta_{1 ^{\ast}$ in $\{0, 0.1,\ldots,1\}$ to control the signal strength.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: When $\beta_{1 ^{\ast =0$, the null hypothesis is true.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Here, we reject the null hypothesis at significance level $\alpha=0.05$.
elements.sent-2411-227: The other settings remain the same as in Study 1.
elements.sent-2411-228: The empirical powers are depicted in Figure \ref{fig:power .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \captionsetup{font = footnotesize} \subfigure[$(n,p) = (200, 500)$, $\varepsilon\sim \calN(0,1)$]{ \begin{minipage}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/power_normal500.pdf} \end{minipage} } \subfigure[$(n,p) = (200, 2000)$, $\varepsilon\sim \calN(0,1)$]{ \begin{minipage}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/power_normal2000.pdf} \end{minipage} } \subfigure[$(n,p) = (200, 500)$, $\varepsilon\sim t_{2}$]{ \begin{minipage}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/power_t500.pdf} \end{minipage} } \subfigure[$(n,p) = (200, 2000)$, $\varepsilon\sim t_{2}$]{ \begin{minipage}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/power_t2000.pdf} \end{minipage} } \subfigure[$(n,p) = (200, 500)$, $\varepsilon\sim Cauchy(0,1)$]{ \begin{minipage}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/power_cauchy500.pdf} \end{minipage} } \subfigure[$(n,p) = (200, 2000)$, $\varepsilon\sim Cauchy(0,1)$]{ \begin{minipage}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/power_cauchy2000.pdf} \end{minipage} } \subfigure{ \begin{minipage}{0.85\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{fig/legend.pdf} \end{minipage} } \caption{ The empirical powers of six test statistics: partial Gini covariance based tests ($\pGcov$); partial quantile covariance based tests ($\pQcov$); partial Pearson covariance based tests ($\pPcov$); modified partial Pearson covariance based tests ($\pPcov_{m}$); de-biased Lasso based tests ($\dBeta$); de-biased Lasso based tests using bootstrap ($\dBeta_{b}$). The horizontal axis represents the signal strength $\beta_{1}^{\ast}$. } \label{fig:power}
elements.sent-2411-229: From Figure \ref{fig:power , we observe that as the signal strength $\beta_{1 ^{\ast}$ increases, all the tests demonstrate improved power performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-230: Notably, when the noise follows the standard normal distribution, the partial Gini covariance-based test shows power performance comparable to the partial Pearson-based tests and two debiased Lasso-based tests.
elements.sent-2411-231: The power of the test based on partial quantile covariance grows slowly as $\beta^{\ast _{1}$ increases, compared with the other five methods.}
elements.sent-2411-232: This phenomenon can be attributed to equation \eqref{equation:are_quantile , which quantifies Pitman's efficiency of partial quantile covariance.}
elements.sent-2411-233: When noise follows $t$ or Cauchy distribution, the power of our proposed partial Gini covariance outperforms the other five methods in terms of power.
elements.sent-2411-234: The simulation results echo the discussion about asymptotic relative efficiency in equation \eqref{equation:are .}
elements.sent-2411-235: In addition, Figure \ref{fig:power illustrates that the two partial Pearson covariance tests and two de-biased Lasso tests display similar empirical power performance.}
elements.sent-2411-236: We investigate the empirical sizes and powers of chi-square test statistics based on multivariate partial Gini covariance, with the same high-dimensional model as discussed in the previous subsection.
elements.sent-2411-237: In this study, we test the hypothesis $H_{0,\calS}$.
elements.sent-2411-238: To be specific, we consider two cases:.
elements.sent-2411-239: In the first case, we test $3$ elements, with all elements greater than $0$ under the alternative hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-240: The second case represents a sparse scenario, where only 3 out of the 10 elements are non-zero.
elements.sent-2411-241: We vary $\beta=\beta_{1 ^{\ast = \beta_{2 ^{\ast = \beta_{3 ^{\ast}$ in $\{0, 0.1,\ldots,1\}$ to control the dependence level.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Specifically, when $\beta=0$, the null hypothesis holds true.
elements.sent-2411-243: The empirical sizes and powers are reported in Table \ref{table:multi_sizepower .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Table \ref{table:multi_sizepower confirms that the sizes of proposed methods are well-controlled in both cases, given the fact that all the empirical sizes are around $0.05$ when $\beta=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-245: When $\beta>0$, we can find that the empirical power has better performance as the dependence strength level $\beta$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-246: The empirical powers of our tests approach $1$ as $\beta$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-247: In this section, we apply our methods to a car pricing dataset.
elements.sent-2411-248: Our goal is to identify the variables that are significant in predicting the car price.
elements.sent-2411-249: The dataset comes from market surveys conducted across the United States by the Chinese automobile company Geely Auto and is available for download from \url{https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/goyalshalini93/car-data .}
elements.sent-2411-250: The data comprises $n=205$ different cars with $25$ variables including the price and other $24$ variables related to the car characteristic.
elements.sent-2411-251: We remove $5$ description variables such as car names and focus on the remaining $19$ predictors.
elements.sent-2411-252: We consider six methods, as discussed in Section \ref{subsec:4.1 , which include partial Gini covariance, quantile partial covariance with $\tau = 0.5$, partial Pearson covariance, and partial Pearson covariance modified-based test statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-253: To transform the real dataset into a high-dimensional one and further examine our methods in controlling type I error, we artificially create $380$ variables obtained by $20$ different permutations of the row order of the original $19$ predictors.
elements.sent-2411-254: This results in a dimensionality of $p = 380+19 = 399$.
elements.sent-2411-255: For each true covariate, we calculate the partial covariances-based test statistics and the corresponding $p$-values.
elements.sent-2411-256: To examine whether our methods can control the empirical size, we set different significance levels $\alpha\in\{0.01, 0.05, 0.1\}$ and report the rejection proportions among $380$ simulated variables in Table \ref{table:realdata_size .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \captionsetup{font = footnotesize} \caption{ The rejection proportions among $380$ simulated variables at significance levels $\alpha\in\{0.01, 0.5, 0.1\}$. Six independence test statistics include: partial Gini covariance ($\pGcov$), partial quantile covariance ($\pQcov$) with $\tau=0.5$, partial Pearson covariance ($\pPcov$), modified partial Pearson covariance ($\pPcov_{m}$), de-biased Lasso ($\dBeta$), and de-biased Lasso using bootstrap based tests ($\dBeta_{b}$). } \label{table:realdata_size} 1{ \begin{tabular}{lrrr|lrrr} $\alpha$ 0.01 0.05 0.1 0.01 0.05 0.1 $\pGcov$ 0.011 0.053 0.105 $\pQcov$ 0.008 0.061 0.113 $\pPcov$ 0.003 0.024 0.061 $\pPcov_{m}$ 0.008 0.042 0.089 $\dBeta$ 0.008 0.037 0.076 $\dBeta_{b}$ 0.011 0.031 0.076 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-257: From the table, we can see that the rejection proportions of partial Gini covariance and partial quantile covariance are around the significance levels, which indicates that these two methods work well in controlling type I errors.
elements.sent-2411-258: In contrast, tests based on partial Pearson covariance and debiased Lasso tend to be conservative.
elements.sent-2411-259: This is mainly due to the heavy-tailed distribution of the response variable.
elements.sent-2411-260: To further identify the variables that are significant in predicting the price, we report the test statistics and corresponding $p$-values for $19$ original variables in Table \ref{table:realdata_pvalue .}
elements.sent-2411-261: From the results in Table \ref{table:realdata_pvalue , we observe that our method has selected $7$ variables with $p$-values less than $0.05$.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Notably, these $7$ variables include the $4$ variables selected by partial quantile covariance and $2$ by modified partial Pearson covariance.
elements.sent-2411-263: This phenomenon indicates that our method has larger power in testing compared with other methods, which echoes the theoretical and simulation results in Section \ref{sec:4 .}
elements.sent-2411-264: Due to the heavy-tailed distribution of the response, the partial Pearson covariance-based test does not work well and has inconsistent results with the previously mentioned three methods.
elements.sent-2411-265: The two debiased Lasso methods, despite employing the same test statistics, produce distinct $p$-values.
elements.sent-2411-266: This inconsistency indicates the price follows heavy-tailed distribution and the debiased Lasso methods are not applicable.
elements.sent-2411-267: We further conduct our partial Gini chi-square test to examine its performance when the coefficients are multivariate.
elements.sent-2411-268: Specifically, we consider three different sets: (1) $\calS_{1 = \{\text{``curbweight" , \text{``horsepower" , \text{``citympg"}\}$ includes variables whose $p$-value is less than $0.01$ in pratial Gini covariance test.; (2) $\calS_{2 = \{\text{``enginelocation" , \text{``carlength" , \text{``carwidth" , \text{``curbweight" , $ $ \text{``cylindernumber" , \text{``horsepower" , \text{``citympg"}\}$ includes variables whose $p$-value is less than $0.05$ in pratial Gini covariance test.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: We report the test statistics and corresponding $p$-values of these three tests in Table \ref{table:realdata_multi , with all three $p$-values less than $0.001$.}
elements.sent-2411-270: This implies that among all three subsets of variables, at least one is significant in predicting the car price, which is consistent with the results shown above.
elements.figure-2411-007: \captionsetup{font = footnotesize} \caption{ The partial Gini covariance chi-square test statistics ($W_{G}$) and their corresponding $p$-values. } \label{table:realdata_multi} 1{ \begin{tabular}{lrrr} $\calS_{1}$ $\calS_{2}$ $\calS_{3}$ $W_{G}$ 42.530 57.515 163.813 $p$-values $<$0.001 $<$0.001 $<$0.001 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-271: For rank Lasso, we verify that \eqref{equation:se holds with high probability in the Supplementary Material.}
